metadata.notes: Content overhaul to Wingspan standard — Rogue B, March 2026. Major corrections from official rulebook, BGG FAQ, RulePop interactive rulebook.

key_rulings.text: When you Lead: deploy a card to a location (trigger its deploy ability), then collect the TOP card from a DIFFERENT location and gain that location's bonus — OR draw from the deck and roll the Rising die.
When you Scout: reveal the top card of the deck and place (NOT deploy — no ability triggers) it face-up on any location. Gain that location's bonus.
Deploy triggers the card's deploy ability. Place does NOT. Only the Lead action's Step 1 triggers deploy abilities. Scout and die effects use Place.
The Sovereign token is worth 10 VP at game end. Gained by collecting from Luna.
Each Helium token is worth 3 VP at game end.
Influence at The Institute: most tokens = 4 VP each, second most = 2 VP each, all others = 1 VP each.
At game end, lose 10 VP for each card in your hand beyond 7.
The game ends when any single player meets 2 of the 3 conditions, OR when any combination of 3 conditions are met across all players. Conditions: Fleet at 7+, Influence 7+, Helium 7+.
When you collect from Luna: gain/keep the Sovereign token, advance 1 on Fleet, gain 1 Helium, roll the Rising die, and trigger your House ability.
When collecting during Lead, take only the TOP card from the chosen location — not all cards.

subtopics.title: Components
subtopics.content:
#### Cards You'll Use
You'll find **112 character cards** (65x100mm) in the box. Each one belongs to one of 14 Colors from the Red Rising universe, and every card shows three things you'll care about: a **core value** (the number in the upper-left corner), a **deploy ability** (the middle text that triggers when you play the card to the board), and an **end-game bonus** (the bottom text that scores extra if you meet its condition when the game ends). You'll also get **6 reference cards** summarizing turn actions and end-game triggers, plus **25 Automa cards** for solo play.

#### The Board
Your game board (410x284mm) shows the 4 locations you'll deploy cards to and collect from: **Jupiter**, **Mars**, **Luna**, and **The Institute**. Each location has a card stack area and a bonus icon. Running along the edge of the board is the **Fleet Track** (positions 0 through 14), where you'll race your Fleet token for progressive VP.

#### Tokens
You'll handle several types of tokens throughout the game:
- **60 Helium tokens** — red gem-shaped pieces. These are worth **3 VP each** at game end, and they pile up fast.
- **60 Influence tokens** (10 per player in 6 colors) — you'll place these at The Institute and score them through tiered majority at game end.
- **6 Fleet tokens** — one per player, tracking your position on the Fleet Track.
- **1 Sovereign token** — a large ornate token worth **10 VP** at game end. This changes hands throughout the game whenever someone collects from Luna. Holding it also triggers your House ability.
- **1 Rising token** — the crescent-moon first-player marker.
- **1 custom Rising die** (22mm) — six faces that can swing your turn: Jupiter (advance Fleet), Mars (gain Helium), Luna (gain Sovereign), Institute (place Influence), Banish (remove a card from the game), and Reveal/Place (flip a deck card onto the board).

#### House Tiles
Each player draws **1 of 6 House tiles** at setup. Your House gives you an asymmetric ability that triggers whenever you gain the Sovereign token:
- **Apollo** — you take the first AND last turn of the game. When you gain Sovereign, reveal and place the top deck card.
- **Ceres** — you start with 6 cards instead of 5. When you gain Sovereign, banish 1 card from a location. You must banish 1 card from your hand before scoring.
- **Diana** — when you gain Sovereign, place 1 Influence at The Institute.
- **Jupiter** — when you gain Sovereign, advance 1 extra on the Fleet Track.
- **Mars** — when you gain Sovereign, gain 1 extra Helium.
- **Minerva** — when you gain Sovereign, roll the Rising die for an extra bonus.

#### Other Bits
- 1 wolf-head card tray and lid for the deck
- 1 scorepad for end-game tallying
- 2 rulebooks (multiplayer and solo)

subtopics.title: Game Structure & Setup
subtopics.content:
#### How the Game Works
Red Rising is a **hand-management combo game** where your hand of cards at the end IS your score. Every card has a core value plus conditional end-game bonuses, so you're constantly trying to sculpt the perfect collection. On each turn, you either **Lead** (deploy a card from your hand to a location, triggering its ability, then collect a different card and a location bonus) or **Scout** (reveal a deck card to a location and gain its bonus without giving up anything from your hand). The game ends when enough end-game conditions are triggered across the table.

One critical rule to keep in mind from the start: **you lose 10 VP for every card beyond 7 in your hand.** Your hand can grow, but excess cards are punished hard.

#### Setting Up
1. **Shuffle all 112 character cards** into a face-down deck and place it in the card tray.
2. **Place 2 face-up cards** at each of the 4 locations (8 cards total on the board).
3. **Deal 5 cards** to each player as their starting hand (6 if you drew House Ceres).
4. **Each player draws 1 random House tile** and takes their 10 matching Influence tokens.
5. **Place all Fleet tokens** on position 0 of the Fleet Track.
6. **Place the Helium supply** near the board where everyone can reach it.
7. **Place the Sovereign token** beside the board — nobody owns it yet.
8. **Give the Rising token** to the Apollo player (if in play), or choose a starting player randomly.
9. **Place the Rising die** near the board.

#### Checkpoint
Before your first turn, confirm you have: 5 cards in hand (6 for Ceres), 10 Influence tokens in your personal supply, your Fleet token on position 0, a House tile in front of you, and a reference card. The board should show 2 face-up cards at each of the 4 locations. The Sovereign token and Rising die sit nearby, unclaimed.

subtopics.title: Turn Actions: Lead & Scout
subtopics.content:
#### Your Turn
On your turn, you do exactly one of two things: **Lead** or **Scout**.

#### Action 1: Lead (Deploy, Then Collect)
This is your main action for swapping cards and building your hand.

**Step 1 — Deploy a card.** Pick 1 card from your hand and place it face-up **on top** of any of the 4 locations. That card's **deploy ability** (the middle text) triggers immediately. Deploy abilities do things like draw cards, gain Helium, advance Fleet, place Influence, banish cards, or chain other effects. If a card's ability conditions aren't met, you can still deploy it — the ability simply doesn't resolve.

**Step 2 — Collect.** Now choose ONE of these options:
- **(a) Collect from a location:** Take the **top card** from a **different** location (not the one you just deployed to) and add it to your hand. Then gain that location's bonus.
- **(b) Draw from the deck:** Draw the top card of the deck into your hand. Then **roll the Rising die** and gain whatever it shows.

Here's what each location gives you when you collect from it:
- **Jupiter** — advance your Fleet token **1 space** on the Fleet Track.
- **Mars** — gain **1 Helium** from the supply.
- **Luna** — the jackpot. You gain ALL of the following: the **Sovereign token** (take it from whoever has it), **+1 Fleet**, **+1 Helium**, a **Rising die roll**, and your **House tile ability** triggers.
- **The Institute** — place **1 Influence token** from your supply onto The Institute.

#### Action 2: Scout
Scouting is your move when your hand is already strong and you don't want to give up any card.

1. **Reveal** the top card of the deck.
2. **Place** it face-up on top of any of the 4 locations. This is a PLACE, not a deploy — the card's deploy ability does **NOT** trigger.
3. **Gain that location's bonus** (same bonuses listed above).

You still advance on tracks and gain resources through Scouting — you just don't sacrifice any hand quality to do it.

#### Deploy vs. Place — The Critical Distinction
This trips up new players, so pay close attention:
- **Deploy** = your card, from your hand, and its deploy ability triggers.
- **Place** = a card goes to a location but its ability does NOT trigger.

Only the Lead action's Step 1 deploys. Scout and Rising die effects always place. If you mix these up, you'll accidentally trigger abilities that shouldn't fire.

subtopics.title: Colors, Die & Resources
subtopics.content:
#### Card Colors — What Matters for Play
Every card belongs to one of 14 Colors, but you don't need to memorize all of them to play well. Here are the ones with direct mechanical significance:

**The two wild Colors** — these are your best friends for scoring:
- **Orange** cards act as **wild for character names**. If an end-game bonus on one of your cards requires a specific named character, any Orange card in your hand satisfies it.
- **Gray** cards act as **wild for colors**. If a bonus requires a card of a specific Color, any Gray card counts.

Even a single Orange or Gray card can unlock bonus conditions across multiple other cards in your hand. Always keep an eye out for them.

**Colors tied to specific tracks and locations:**
- **Blue** cards advance your Fleet when deployed. If you're pushing a Jupiter/Fleet strategy, Blue is your core Color.
- **Copper** cards synergize with The Institute and Influence. They pay off when you're competing for Influence majority.
- **Gold** cards carry the highest core values in the game (30-40 range) and powerful end-game bonuses. Landing even one Gold card anchors your score.
- **Silver** cards specialize in the Helium economy — converting, multiplying, or rewarding Helium stockpiles.
- **White** cards reward holding or gaining the Sovereign token.

**The remaining Colors** (Red, Brown, Pink, Obsidian, Yellow, Green, Violet) each have thematic abilities — banishing cards, moving cards between locations, cycling the deck, and set-collection bonuses. You'll learn their nuances as you see them in play, but they don't drive strategy the way the colors above do.

#### The Rising Die
You'll roll this die when you draw from the deck during a Lead action, and whenever Luna or a card effect triggers it. Its six faces are:
1. **Jupiter** — advance 1 on Fleet Track.
2. **Mars** — gain 1 Helium.
3. **Luna** — gain or keep the Sovereign token (and trigger your House ability).
4. **Institute** — place 1 Influence token.
5. **Banish (X)** — remove the top card from any location entirely from the game.
6. **Reveal/Place** — reveal the top deck card and place it on any location.

#### The Sovereign Token
The Sovereign is a single token worth **10 VP** at game end — one of the biggest individual point sources in the game.
- You gain it by collecting from Luna, rolling Luna on the die, or through certain card abilities. If another player holds it, you take it from them.
- Every time you gain the Sovereign (even if you already hold it), your **House tile ability** triggers. This means repeated Luna visits stack your House bonuses.
- The Sovereign also serves as the **tiebreaker** — if final scores are tied, whoever holds it wins.

#### Fleet Track
Your Fleet token moves along positions 0 through 14, with **progressive scoring** printed right on the track (0 VP at position 0, scaling up to **43 VP** at position 14). You advance by collecting from Jupiter, deploying Blue cards, rolling Jupiter on the die, or collecting from Luna. Reaching position 7 or higher is one of the three end-game trigger conditions.

#### Influence Tokens
You start with 10 Influence tokens in your color. Place them at The Institute through the location bonus, card abilities, or the Rising die. At game end, Influence uses **tiered scoring**: the player with the most Influence scores **4 VP per token**, second most scores **2 VP per token**, and everyone else scores **1 VP per token**. That gap between 4 VP and 1 VP per token is enormous — if you're investing in Influence, fight for the majority. Having 7 or more Influence at The Institute is one of the three end-game trigger conditions.

#### Helium
Each Helium token is worth **3 VP** at game end. You gain Helium from Mars, Luna, card abilities, or the Rising die. Some Silver cards can convert Helium into other benefits. Reaching 7 or more Helium tokens is one of the three end-game trigger conditions.

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content:
#### End-Game Triggers
The game ends when **either** of these happens:
- **Any single player** meets **2 of the 3 conditions**, OR
- **All 3 conditions** are met by **any combination of players** (different players can satisfy different conditions).

The three conditions all share the same threshold of **7**:
1. A player has **7+ Helium tokens**.
2. A player has **7+ Influence tokens** at The Institute.
3. A player's **Fleet token** has reached position **7+** on the Fleet Track.

Once triggered, **all players complete the current round** so everyone gets equal turns. If House Apollo is in play, that player takes the final turn of the game.

**There is no deck-exhaustion trigger.** Only Helium, Influence, and Fleet can end the game.

#### Scoring Formula
After any end-game card abilities resolve, tally your score:

1. **Card core values** — sum the upper-left number on every card in your hand.
2. **Card end-game bonuses** — check each card's bottom text. Score bonuses for conditions you've met. Some cards penalize you if their conditions are NOT met, so read carefully.
3. **Fleet Track** — score VP based on your position (0 at position 0, scaling up to 43 at position 14; the progressive scale is printed right on the track).
4. **Helium** — **3 VP** per Helium token you hold.
5. **Sovereign bonus** — **10 VP** if you hold the Sovereign token.
6. **Influence at The Institute** — tiered: most Influence = **4 VP/token**, second most = **2 VP/token**, everyone else = **1 VP/token**.
7. **Excess card penalty** — **-10 VP** for each card beyond 7 in your hand. This is brutal — even one extra card wipes out most of what that card scores.

#### Tiebreaker
If scores are tied, the player holding the Sovereign token wins.

subtopics.title: Beginner Strategy
subtopics.content:
#### Core Principle: Hand Quality Over Quantity
Every card in your hand scores its core value plus potential end-game bonuses. But at **-10 VP per card beyond 7**, a bloated hand is devastating. Aim for 5 to 7 high-synergy cards, not a pile of 10 mediocre ones. If your hand grows past 7, start deploying your weakest cards immediately.

#### Where the Big Points Live
Know your scoring hierarchy so you can focus your efforts:
- **Gold cards** have the highest core values (30-40 range). Even one Gold card can anchor your entire score.
- **Fleet Track** can yield up to 43 VP — aggressive Jupiter and Blue strategies are some of the strongest in the game.
- **Sovereign token** is a flat 10 VP, plus it triggers your House ability every time you gain it. If nobody is contesting Luna, that's free points.
- **Helium** at 3 VP each adds up fast. Five Helium tokens is 15 VP for doing nothing special.
- **Influence** is tiered — having the MOST tokens at The Institute makes each one worth 4 VP instead of 1 VP. That's a 4x difference for winning the majority.

#### Orange and Gray: Your Best Support Colors
- **Orange** cards act as **wild for character names** — they satisfy end-game bonus conditions that reference specific characters you might not have.
- **Gray** cards act as **wild for colors** — they satisfy conditions requiring cards of specific Colors.

Holding even one Orange or one Gray can unlock bonus conditions across multiple other cards in your hand. When you spot them on a location, prioritize picking them up.

#### House-Color Synergies
Your House tile points you toward a natural strategy. Lean into it:
- **House Jupiter + Blue cards** — Blues advance Fleet on deploy, and Jupiter's House ability adds +1 Fleet when you gain Sovereign. Stack these for massive Fleet Track VP.
- **House Diana + Copper cards** — Diana places Influence when you gain Sovereign, and Copper cards synergize with The Institute. Push for Influence majority at 4 VP per token.
- **House Mars + Silver cards** — Mars gains extra Helium when you gain Sovereign, and Silvers convert and multiply Helium. Stockpile Helium for 3 VP each.
- **House Apollo + White cards** — Apollo gets the first and last turns. Whites reward holding the Sovereign. Secure it early for 10 VP plus White card bonuses.

#### When to Lead vs. Scout
- **Lead** is usually the stronger play — you gain a location bonus AND a new card, at the cost of deploying one card from your hand.
- **Scout** shines when your hand is already strong (5-6 great cards) and you don't want to risk giving one up. You still collect a location bonus without sacrificing hand quality.
- **Scouting to Luna** is especially powerful — you get Sovereign, Fleet, Helium, a die roll, and your House ability, all without losing a single card.

#### Watching End-Game Triggers
Keep an eye on everyone's Fleet, Influence, and Helium. The game ends when 1 player hits 2 of 3 conditions, or all 3 conditions are met across any combination of players.
- **Don't trigger the end before you're ready.** If your hand still needs work, avoid pushing past 7 on any track.
- **If an opponent is close to triggering**, either race to finish your combos or deliberately slow their path by competing for the same resources.

#### Common Mistakes
- **Holding 8+ cards.** Each card beyond 7 costs you 10 VP. That's more than most cards are worth. Deploy your weakest cards aggressively to stay lean.
- **Deploying Gold cards.** A 35-point Gold deployed for a Mars bonus (3 VP in Helium) is a 32-VP loss. Always deploy your lowest-value cards instead.
- **Ignoring the Sovereign token.** 10 VP is massive, and it triggers your House ability every time. If nobody is contesting Luna, Scout there repeatedly.
- **Spreading across all 14 Colors.** Focus on 2-3 Colors that synergize with your House tile, and use Orange and Gray as wild support to fill gaps.
- **Confusing deploy and place.** Scout and die effects PLACE cards — no deploy abilities trigger. Only the Lead action's Step 1 deploys. Getting this wrong means activating abilities that shouldn't fire.

subtopics.title: First-Game Walkthrough
subtopics.content:
#### Your First Game of Red Rising

**After Setup**: Look at your hand — you have 5 cards (6 if you drew House Ceres). Check your House tile to see what your Sovereign ability does. You also have 10 Influence tokens in your supply and your Fleet token sitting at position 0. Each location on the board has 2 face-up cards. The Sovereign token is unclaimed.

#### Opening Turns

**Turn 1 — Lead**: Find the weakest card in your hand — the one with the lowest core value or a bonus you'll never complete. Deploy it to a location, say Luna. Its deploy ability triggers, so resolve whatever it says. Now collect from a different location. If Mars has a Gold card sitting on top, take it into your hand and gain 1 Helium from the Mars bonus. You just traded a weak card for a strong one and picked up a resource.

**Turn 2 — Scout to Luna**: Your hand is looking solid and you don't want to lose any of it. Reveal the top deck card and place it on Luna (remember, placing does NOT trigger the card's deploy ability). Now collect Luna's bonus package: take the Sovereign token (10 VP at game end), advance 1 on Fleet, gain 1 Helium, roll the Rising die, and trigger your House ability. All of that without giving up a single card.

**Turns 3-5 — Build Your Combos**: Keep deploying your weakest cards and collecting stronger ones from locations. Watch for Orange and Gray cards on the board — they act as wilds for your end-game bonus conditions and can unlock points across multiple cards in your hand. Keep your hand at or below 7 cards to dodge the -10 VP penalty.

#### Mid-Game

By now you should have a sense of your strategy. Check the board state: which end-game conditions are approaching 7? If your Fleet is at 5, two more Jupiter collections will trigger that condition. If your hand has strong combos ready to score, you WANT the game to end. If your hand still needs work, slow down and keep sculpting instead of pushing tracks.

#### Late Game — Lock In Your Score

Stop deploying anything valuable. Scout if your hand is strong. Make sure your end-game bonus conditions are actually met — check whether your Orange and Gray wilds complete any requirements on your other cards. Count your hand size and deploy down to 7 if needed.

#### End-Game Trigger
The game ends when 1 player hits 2 of 3 conditions (Fleet 7+, Influence 7+, Helium 7+), or when all 3 conditions are met across any combination of players. There is no deck-exhaustion trigger. Once triggered, everyone finishes the current round for equal turns.

#### Scoring
Grab the scorepad and add up: card core values + end-game bonuses + Fleet Track VP + (Helium x 3) + Sovereign (10 VP if you hold it) + Influence (tiered: 4/2/1 VP per token based on majority). Then subtract 10 VP for each card beyond 7 in your hand. For your first game, 80-120 VP is a competitive score.

subtopics.title: Advanced Strategy
subtopics.content:
#### Hand Sculpting
Red Rising is fundamentally about building the perfect 5-7 card hand. Every deploy-and-collect decision should ask one question: "Does this improve my end-game hand?" A powerful card sitting on the board is only useful if you can collect it before someone else does or the game ends.

- **Set a deploy ceiling: never deploy a card worth more than the benefit you gain.** Deploying a 35-point Gold for a 3 VP Helium bonus is a 32-VP loss. Always deploy your lowest-value cards.
- **Hand trimming matters more than you think.** At 8 cards, you're eating -10 VP. At 9, that's -20. Actively deploy down to 7 or below, even if it means losing a decent card — the math almost always favors a leaner hand.

#### Color Synergy Stacking
- **Gold + Orange/Gray:** Gold cards have the highest core values (30-40) and demanding end-game conditions. Orange (wild for character names) and Gray (wild for colors) make those conditions dramatically easier to meet. One Gray card can unlock bonuses across multiple Gold cards simultaneously.
- **Blue + Jupiter location:** Blue cards advance Fleet on deploy. Combine with Jupiter collections for rapid Fleet advancement. Fleet position 10+ is worth 25+ VP — that's your payoff for committing to this track.
- **Copper + Institute majority:** Copper cards synergize with Influence. If you're placing 5-6 Influence tokens, you want majority (4 VP per token) — that's 20-24 VP from Influence alone versus 5-6 VP at the bottom tier.
- **Silver + Helium stacking:** Silver cards convert and multiply Helium. Pair them with House Mars (extra Helium when gaining Sovereign) and you can stockpile 8-10 Helium for 24-30 VP.

#### The Sovereign Token Economy
The Sovereign token is worth 10 VP at game end, and it triggers your House ability every time you gain it. That combination makes Luna the most valuable location in the game.

- **Collecting from Luna gives you everything:** Sovereign (10 VP), +1 Fleet, +1 Helium (3 VP), a die roll, and your House ability. That's potentially 15+ VP from a single collection.
- **House abilities compound with repeated Sovereign gains.** House Jupiter gets +1 Fleet each time. House Mars gets +1 Helium each time. House Diana places Influence each time. Gaining the Sovereign 3-4 times across a game stacks these bonuses into serious point totals.
- **If nobody contests Luna, Scout there every turn.** You gain all of Luna's bonuses without losing a card from your hand. This is one of the strongest repeatable plays in the game.

#### Location Mastery
- **Jupiter** — advance Fleet. Your go-to when running a Blue-heavy strategy or racing toward Fleet 7+ for the end-game trigger.
- **Mars** — gain Helium (3 VP each). Best for Silver strategies or when you're stacking toward the Helium 7+ trigger.
- **Luna** — the full package. Best overall location but highly contested in multiplayer. Worth fighting for because of the Sovereign and the bonus stack.
- **The Institute** — place Influence. Best for Copper strategies chasing majority scoring. Less impactful if you've fallen behind in the Influence race.

#### End-Game Trigger Manipulation
The game ends when 1 player hits 2 of 3 conditions (Fleet 7+, Influence 7+, Helium 7+), or when all 3 conditions are met across any combination of players.

- **Trigger when you're ahead.** If your hand scores 120+ and opponents are still building, push past 7 on two tracks to force the end before they catch up.
- **Stall when you're behind.** If your combos aren't complete, avoid advancing past 7 on any track. Let opponents trigger the end — you'll still get to finish the round.
- **Watch the collective threshold.** Even if no single player has 2 conditions, the game ends when all 3 are met by any combination. One player at Fleet 7, another at Helium 7, and a third at Influence 7 ends the game — even though nobody individually triggered it.

#### Player Count Adjustments
- **2-3 players:** Locations stay stacked with cards, giving you more to choose from. The Sovereign changes hands less often, and engine strategies have more time to develop.
- **4-6 players:** Locations get stripped quickly. Luna is fiercely contested. End-game triggers fire faster because more players are pushing tracks. Prioritize hand quality early — you may not get as many turns as you expect.
